paper_title: ARISE: Agent Reasoning with Intrinsic Skill Evolution in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The dominant paradigm for improving mathematical reasoning in language models relies on Reinforcement Learning with verifiable rewards. Yet existing methods treat each problem instance in isolation without leveraging the reusable strategies that emerge and accumulate during training. To this end, we introduce ARISE (Agent Reasoning via Intrinsic Skill Evolution), a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework, in which a shared policy operates both to manage skills at high-level and to generate responses at low-level (denoted as a Skills Manager and a Worker, respectively). The Manager maintains a tiered skill library through a dedicated skill generation rollout that performs structured summarization of successful solution traces (after execution), while employing a policy-driven selection mechanism to retrieve relevant skills to condition future rollouts (before execution). A hierarchical reward design guides the co-evolution of reasoning ability and library quality. Experiments on two base models and seven benchmarks spanning both competition mathematics and Omni-MATH show that ARISE consistently outperforms GRPO-family algorithms and memory-augmented baselines, with particularly notable gains on out-of-distribution tasks. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes to the observed improvements and that library quality and reasoning performance improve in tandem throughout training. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Skylanding/ARISE}{https://github.com/Skylanding/ARISE}.

sections.content: 6 2 0 2 7 1 ] . [ 1 0 6 0 6 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r Preprint. Under review. ARISE: Agent Reasoning with Intrinsic Skill Evolution in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Yu Li1, Rui Miao2, Zhengling Qi3, Tian Lan1 1Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, George Washington University 2Department of Mathematical Sciences, University of Texas at Dallas 3School of Business, George Washington University {yul, tlan, qizhengling}@gwu.edu, rui.miao@utdallas.edu

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The dominant paradigm for improving mathematical reasoning in language models relies on Reinforcement Learning with verifiable rewards. Yet existing methods treat each problem instance in isolation without leveraging the reusable strategies that emerge and accumulate during training. To this end, we introduce ARISE (Agent Reasoning via Intrinsic Skill Evolution), hierarchical reinforcement learning framework, in which shared policy operates both to manage skills at high-level and to generate responses at low-level (denoted as Skills Manager and Worker, respectively). The Manager maintains tiered skill library through dedicated skill generation rollout that performs structured summarization of successful solution traces (after execution), while employing policy-driven selection mechanism to retrieve relevant skills to condition future rollouts (before execution). hierarchical reward design guides the co-evolution of reasoning ability and library quality. Experiments on two base models and seven benchmarks spanning both competition mathematics and Omni-MATH show that ARISE consistently outperforms GRPO-family algorithms and memory-augmented baselines, with particularly notable gains on out-of-distribution tasks. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes to the observed improvements and that library quality and reasoning performance improve in tandem throughout training. Code is available at https://github.com/Skylanding/ARISE.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has emerged as compelling paradigm for training mathematical reasoning in large language models, enabling policies to improve through trial-and-error without relying on expensive human annotation (Guo et al., 2025). Despite strong performance on standard benchmarks, existing methods solve each problem instance via separate process: Once rollout concludes, the successful reasoning strategies generated in the process are discarded rather than retained and accumulated for future use (Sun et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025). natural remedy is to equip the agent with persistent skill library that accumulates reusable reasoning strategies over time (Wang et al., 2025; Wei et al., 2026). Recent work has demonstrated the value of organizing past experience into structured skills and retrieving them at inference or training time (Wu et al., 2025; Xia et al., 2026; Wang et al., 2025), allowing agents to accumulate and transfer knowledge across problems and avoid redundant exploration. However, existing approaches share fundamental limitation: skill management, including both skill selection before execution and skill summarization after execution, is delegated to an external retriever, preventing the policy gradient from directly shaping skill selection. Furthermore, the skill library is updated independently of the RL objective, breaking the feedback loop between policy improvement and library enrichment (Hao et al., 2024). 1 Preprint. Under review. Figure 1: Overview of ARISE on Qwen3-4B, showing performance across seven benchmarks, training reward dynamics on DeepScaleR, skill utilization over training, and accuracy gain versus token overhead relative to GRPO. We present ARISE, an integrated, hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for Agent Reasoning via Intrinsic Skill Evolution, that addresses both limitations through unified design. The key insight is that the skill library should not be static external resource but an intrinsic component of the agents state, co-evolving with the policy throughout training. ARISErealizes Manager-Worker hierarchy within single shared policy πθ: the manager selects skills using the policys own log-probabilities and generates new skills by summarizing successful solution traces, while the worker generates solution traces conditioned on the selected skill. Since the same parameters govern both skill selection and solution generation, the advantage signal from the hierarchical reward propagates end-to-end, reinforcing selection preferences for skills that demonstrably improve reasoning outcomes. To incentivize skill utilization, we introduce hierarchical reward {r0, r1, r2} with r2 > r1 > r0, distinguishing correct solutions with skill augmentation (r2), without (r1), and incorrect solutions (r0). Under group-relative advantage, this differential signal steers the policy toward consistently leveraging useful skills. The skill library adopts two-tier cachereservoir architecture with five management operations, maintaining compact active pool while preserving skills that may regain relevance as training progresses. Training proceeds in two phases: warm-up that builds the base policy and populates the library via OG+1, followed by skill-augmented phase activating the full hierarchical pipeline. We evaluate ARISE on two instruction-tuned base models, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 (Team, 2025) and Phi-4-mini-instruct (Abouelenin et al., 2025), trained on the DeepScaleR dataset. Results on both in-distribution competition benchmarks and the out-of-distribution benchmark with four mathematical domains (Gao et al., 2024) demonstrate consistent improvements over GRPO-family baselines and existing memoryand skill-augmented methods. Our main contributions are summarized as follows: We propose the Evolving-Skill MDP, formal framework that models the skill library as an endogenous component of the agents state, enabling joint optimization of policy and library under unified RL objective. We introduce policy-driven skill selection mechanism based on conditional logprobability scoring, allowing the policy gradient to directly shape selection preferences end-to-end without relying on an external retriever. We design hierarchical reward and two-tier skill library architecture that together create co-evolutionary dynamic between policy improvement and library enrichment. Preprint. Under review. Empirical results on competition and Olympiad-level benchmarks demonstrate that ARISE consistently outperforms both vanilla GRPO variants and skill-augmented baselines across two base models.

sections.title: 2 Backgrounds
sections.content: Reinforcement learning has emerged as powerful paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Early RLHF methods (Ouyang et al., 2022) relied on learned reward models trained on human preferences, but are susceptible to reward model overoptimization (Gao et al., 2023) and require expensive annotation. Guo et al. (2025) introduced Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), critic-free variant of PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) that estimates advantages via normalized rewards within group of rollouts per query, giving rise to the Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) paradigm now dominant in mathematical reasoning. Several refinements have since addressed GRPOs limitations: Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025) corrects normalization biases in advantage estimation; DAPO (Yu et al., 2025) introduces asymmetric clipping and dynamic sampling to mitigate entropy collapse; and GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025) replaces per-token importance ratios with sequence-level correction to better align with the reward signal. complementary line of research augments LLM agents with external memory or reusable skill structures to enable experience transfer across episodes. Inference-time methods such as Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023), ExpeL (Zhao et al., 2024), and SimpleMem (Liu et al., 2026) retrieve past trajectories or distilled knowledge into the agents context, but the resulting memory is populated independently of policy learning and remains fixed once constructed. More recent work integrates skill structures directly into the training loop: EvolveR (Wu et al., 2025) maintains co-evolving skill library and SkillRL (Xia et al., 2026) builds hierarchical skill bank via trajectory distillation. Building on this direction, SAGE (Wang et al., 2025) further incorporates skill augmentation into GRPO through sequential rollouts, coupling skill generation with policy optimization in unified framework.

sections.title: 3 ARISE: Hierarchical Agent RL with Evolving Skills
sections.content: We present ARISE, hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that addresses two limitations of existing skill-augmented approaches: skill selection is delegated to an external retriever decoupled from the policy gradient, and the skill library is updated independently of the RL objective. As illustrated in Figure 2, ARISE resolves both issues through unified Manager-Worker architecture in which single policy πθ governs all skill interactions. Through Download channel, the manager retrieves relevant skill to condition the workers rollouts and through an Upload channel, the manager distills successful solution traces into new skill documents. Five management operations (ADD, UPDATE, EVICT, LOAD, DELETE) maintain library quality throughout training, ensuring that the library co-evolves with the policy under shared RL objective. We formalize this coupling as an evolving-skill MDP with hierarchical policy (3.1), then describe the two-phase training framework (3.2). 3.1 Evolving-Skill MDP and Hierarchical Policy Skill library agents (Wang et al., 2023; Nguyen et al., 2024) have shown that equipping agents with reusable, structured skills improves their ability to handle specialized tasks. Bringing this paradigm into mathematical reasoning under RL training introduces fundamental challenge: the library is not given priori but must be constructed and refined by the agent itself, coupling library dynamics with policy optimization. To formalize this coupling, we define an Evolving-Skill MDP (ES-MDP) as the tuple Q, M, A, , R, where is the query distribution, is the space of library configurations, is the action space covering skill management operations, : is Preprint. Under review. Figure 2: Overview of ARISE. The shared policy πθ operates as both Skills Manager and Worker. Before each rollout, the manager scores cache entries via conditional log-probability and injects the selected skill into the prompt (Download). After reward computation, an additional rollout OG+1 distills successful solutions into structured skill document (Upload). The two-tier library consists of compact cache (active pool for selection) and larger reservoir (archive for future promotion), maintained by five operations: ADD, UPDATE, EVICT, LOAD, and DELETE. the library transition function, and : {r0, r1, r2} with r2 > r1 > r0 is the hierarchical reward. The augmented state at step is st = (qt, Mt), where qt is sampled exogenously and Mt is the library state shaped by the agents preceding actions. Each library entry ek = (mk, uk) Mt pairs skill document mk with scalar utility estimate uk maintained via exponential moving average. Decision-making follows Manager-Worker hierarchy realized by shared policy πθ. The manager governs skill selection before task execution and skill generation after; the worker generates the solution trace τt. The joint probability over skill management actions at and solution trace τt factorizes as: πθ(at, τt qt, Mt) = πmgr θ (zt qt, Mt) πwkr θ (τt qt, mzt ) πlib θ (alib qt, Mt, τt) (1) where the three factors correspond to skill selection, solution generation, and skill generation with library update, respectively. The component πlib θ generates new skill document conditioned on the query and positive-advantage traces τ+ from the preceding rollouts. All three components share parameters θ but operate under different conditioning contexts. The hierarchical policy operates through three interconnected components: Skill Selection and Solution Generation. Unlike prior approaches that delegate skill retrieval to an external model, ARISE performs selection through πθ itself. For each candidate skill mk Mt, the policy scores query-skill relevance via conditional logprobability: sk = mk l=1 log πθ(m (l) qt, (<l) ) (2) The manager converts these scores into selection distribution and samples from an ε-greedy mixture: pk = exp(sk/σ) exp(sj/σ) , zt (1 ε) δarg maxk pk + ε Uniform(Mt) (3) where σ is temperature parameter. To prevent injection of marginally relevant skills, confidence gate admits the selected skill only when maxk pk δ; otherwise the worker Preprint. Under review. solves the query unaided. When skill passes the gate, its document is prepended to the input context and the worker generates trace τt conditioned on the augmented prompt. Because the same parameters θ govern both selection and generation, the advantage signal from the hierarchical reward propagates end-to-end through the selection mechanism. Skill Generation and Library Management. Beyond the solution rollouts, the manager executes dedicated skill generation rollout OG+1, conditioned on the original query together with the positive-advantage traces τ+ = {τi ˆAi > 0}. The grounding in concrete successful solutions turns skill generation from open-ended strategy induction into structured summarization: πθ extracts reasoning patterns from τ+ into skill document mnew following uniform schema comprising skill name, problem type, key insight, step-by-step method, and verification check. And the uniform format ensures that log-probability scores in Eq. 2 reflect semantic relevance rather than surface variation across skill documents. The skill library adopts two-tier architecture: cache Mc with capacity Cc serves as the active pool for selection, while reservoir Mr with capacity Cr stores surplus skills for future promotion, with Mt = Mc . New skills produced by OG+1 enter the cache via Upload, and selected skills are injected into the workers prompt via Download. Five operations maintain library quality: ADD, UPDATE with uk β uk + (1 β) r, EVICT, LOAD, and DELETE, collectively governed by: Mr Mc t+1, Mr t+1 = (cid:0)Mc , Mr , mnew(cid:1), mnew πθ( qt, τ+) (4) Hierarchical Reward. The reward combines task completion signal rtask {0, 1} with skill utilization bonus rskill 0, granted only when the agent both solves the task and uses selected skill. The composite reward = rtask + rskill {r0, r1, r2} with r2 > r1 > r0 distinguishes correct solutions with skill use (r2), without (r1), and incorrect solutions (r0). Within rollout group containing both r2 and r1 trajectories, group-relative advantage assigns strictly higher values to the skill-augmented ones for any reward structure satisfying r2 > r1 > r0; we set r0=0, r1=1, r2=2 in experiments. As the policy learns to leverage skills more effectively, OG+1 produces higher-quality documents from stronger solution traces, creating co-evolutionary dynamic between policy improvement and library enrichment. 3.2 Two-Phase Training Training proceeds in two phases, summarized in Algorithm 1. In Phase I, the policy is warmed up with standard GRPO on binary task rewards while the skill library is silently populated from successful traces. In Phase II, the full hierarchical pipeline activates: the manager begins selecting skills, the reward switches from rtask {0, 1} to {0, 1, 2}, and policy optimization, skill selection, and library enrichment proceed jointly. Phase I: Warm-Up. The library is initialized with small set of seed skills encoding generic mathematical reasoning heuristics (e.g., extract key quantities, map counting problems to structured objects), following the same schema as generated skills. The library is initialized with small set of seed skills encoding generic mathematical reasoning heuristics (e.g., extract key quantities, map counting problems to structured objects), following the same schema as generated skills. During the first Nw steps, skill selection is disabled and the policy is trained with standard GRPO on the binary reward rtask. Group-relative advantages over rollout trajectories per query are: ˆAi = ri µG σG + ϵ , µG = 1 i=1 ri, σG = (cid:118) (cid:117) (cid:117) (cid:116) 1 G i=1 (ri µG)2 (5) where ri denotes rtask surrogate objective: in Phase and Ri in Phase II. The policy is updated via the clipped LGRPO(θ) = 1 i=1 1 τi τi l=1 min(cid:0)ρi,l ˆAi, clip(ρi,l, 1ϵc, 1+ϵc) ˆAi (cid:1) (6) 5 Preprint. Under review. Algorithm 1 ARISE: Agent Reasoning with Intrinsic Skill Evolution Require: Dataset D, policy πθ0, group size G, warm-up steps Nw, seed skills Mseed 1: Mc Mseed, Mr 2: Phase I: Build base policy and populate skill library 3: for = 1, . . . , Nw do for each do 4: 5: 6: ( q); compute rtask Sample trajectories τi πθold Collect τ+; generate mnew πθ( q, τ+); update Mc, Mr (Eq. 4) skill generation OG+1 end for and ˆAi (Eq. 5) 7: 8: Update θ via LGRPO(θ) (Eq. 6) 9: end for 10: Phase II: Activate full hierarchical pipeline 11: for = Nw + 1, Nw + 2, . . . do 12: 13: 14: for each do 15: 16: Score and select skill zi via Eq. 2, 3 Sample trajectories τi πθold Compute Ri {0, 1, 2} and ˆAi; update uzi β uzi + (1 β) Ri Collect τ+; generate mnew πθ( q, τ+); update Mc, Mr (Eq. 4) skill generation OG+1 end for manager: selection worker: conditioned rollouts ( q, mzi ) 17: 18: Update θ via LGRPO(θ) (Eq. 6) 19: end for (xi,l q, xi,<l) is the per-token importance sampling ratio where ρi,l = πθ(xi,l q, xi,<l) / πθold and ϵc is the clipping parameter. While skill selection remains inactive during warm-up, the skill generation rollout OG+1 executes at every step, summarizing positive-advantage traces τ+ = {τi ˆAi > 0} into structured documents via Eq. 4. Phase II: Skill-Augmented GRPO. Starting from step Nw+1, the manager scores all cache entries via Eq. 2, selects skill through the ε-greedy mechanism of Eq. 3, and the worker generates solutions conditioned on the augmented prompt. The hierarchical reward Ri {0, 1, 2} replaces rtask in the advantage calculation of Eq. 5, and the importance sampling (xi,l ratio now reflects skill-conditioned generation: ρi,l = πθ(xi,l qt, mzi , xi,<l) / πθold qt, mzi , xi,<l). The shift from rtask {0, 1} to {0, 1, 2} directly shapes the policy gradient. Within rollout group containing trajectories that solve the problem both with (R=2) and without (R=1) skill augmentation, the group-relative advantage assigns positive values to skillaugmented trajectories and negative values to unaugmented ones, even though both are correct. i

sections.title: 4 Results
sections.content: 4.1 Implementation Details We train all methods on the DeepScaleR dataset (Luo et al., 2025), comprising approximately 40K problem-answer pairs from AMC, AIME, MATH, and OlympiadBench, using two instruction-tuned base models: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 (Team, 2025) and Phi-4-miniinstruct (Abouelenin et al., 2025). All methods use GRPO with group size G=8 under the same computational budget. We compare against three categories of baselines. GRPO Family (Vanilla) includes GRPO (Guo et al., 2025), Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025), DAPO (Yu et al., 2025), and GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025), representing policy optimization without external knowledge. Memory and Skill-Augmented methods include EvolveR (Wu et al., 2025), SimpleMem (Liu et al., 2026), and SkillRL (Xia et al., 2026), each integrated with GRPO and adapted to our mathematical reasoning setting. We evaluate on two benchmark groups. 6 Preprint. Under review. Table 1: Main results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. We report average Pass@1 accuracy (%) over 32 independent runs. All methods use G=8 and are trained on the DeepScaleR dataset. Bold indicates the best per column within each base model. Shaded rows represent our proposed method, with subscripts indicating absolute change relative to the GRPO baseline. Method Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 Baseline GRPO Family (Vanilla) GRPO (Guo et al., 2025) Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025) DAPO (Yu et al., 2025) GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025) Memory-Augmented EvolveR + GRPO (Wu et al., 2025) SimpleMem + GRPO (Liu et al., 2026) In-Distribution Competition Out-of-Distribution Omni-MATH AMC 23 AIME 24 AIME 25 Algebra Num.Thy Combin. Phi-4-mini-instruct-3.8B Baseline GRPO Family (Vanilla) GRPO (Guo et al., 2025) Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025) DAPO (Yu et al., 2025) GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025) Memory-Augmented EvolveR + GRPO (Wu et al., 2025) SimpleMem + GRPO (Liu et al., 2026) 36.3 9.7 42.7 42.5 44.1 43. 43.2 43.0 10.3 10.6 11.5 11.8 10.9 10.7 4.3 4.7 4.6 6.1 5.0 5.0 4. 10.3 13.2 13.4 14.6 14.2 14.0 13.6 7.1 9.5 9.3 10.7 10.3 10.1 9. 4.4 6.0 6.1 7.0 6.7 6.2 6.4 6.5 8.8 8.6 9.5 9.8 9.4 9. 7.1 9.4 9.4 10.5 10.2 9.9 9.7 ARISE + GRPO 5.91.2 Note: Macro average (arithmetic mean) over four Omni-MATH domains. In-distribution benchmarks share problem types with training data but have no temporal overlap. Omni-MATH sub-categories follow the official domain taxonomy. 10.61. 12.62.3 15.92.7 45.32.6 11.52.0 7.71.7 11.42. In-distribution competition benchmarks (AMC 20231, AIME 2024&20252) share the same problem type as the training set but have no temporal overlap with it. Out-of-distribution Omni-MATH (Gao et al., 2024), comprising 4,428 Olympiad-level problems across Algebra, Number Theory, Combinatorics, and Geometry, assesses generalization beyond the training distribution. All results report average Pass@1 over 32 runs. 4.2 Performance Table 1 summarizes the main results across both base models and all benchmark groups. On Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, ARISE surpasses all baselines across every benchmark, outperforming the strongest GRPO-family methods by over one point on in-distribution tasks. On Phi-4-mini-instruct, where the base model has substantially weaker mathematical ability, ARISE still achieves the highest scores across all benchmarks, confirming that the hierarchical skill mechanism remains effective even when positive training signals are sparse. The advantage of ARISE is more pronounced on out-of-distribution evaluation. On OmniMATH, ARISE improves over GRPO by 2.9 and 1.9 points in average accuracy on the two base models respectively, with gains observed across all four sub-domains. The largest improvements appear in Algebra, where the skill library accumulates the most reusable reasoning patterns. This suggests that the evolving skill library facilitates transfer to unseen mathematical domains rather than merely reinforcing patterns present in the training set. Memory-augmented methods like EvolveR and SimpleMem, improve upon GRPO but do not consistently surpass DAPO or GSPO, occasionally matching them on individual 1https://huggingface.co/datasets/AI-MO/aimo-validation-amc 2https://huggingface.co/datasets/AI-MO/aimo-validation-aime 7 Preprint. Under review. benchmarks while falling short on others. The result reflects their reliance on external retrievers decoupled from the policy gradient. In contrast, ARISE achieves substantially larger gains through end-to-end policy-driven skill selection, where the advantage signal directly shapes which skills are retrieved and retained. Table 2: Ablation on Qwen3-4B with Pass@1 (%). Variant AIME 24 AIME 25 Omni ARISE (full) (a) Binary reward (b) Random select. (c) w/o OG+1 (d) w/o conf. gate 56.4 54.7 55.1 55.0 56.0 49.1 47.1 47.8 47.5 48. 28.0 25.7 26.3 26.0 27.4 4.3 Ablation and Analysis We ablate key design choices of ARISE on Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 (G=8) and report both accuracy and skill library behavior. As shown in Ablation Table, using binary reward causes the largest accuracy drop and reduces skill utilization from 73% to 31%, confirming that the hierarchical signal is the primary driver of skill adoption. Random skill injection maintains high utilization by construction, yet accuracy degrades because mismatched skills provide irrelevant context. Removing the skill generation rollout OG+1 freezes the library at 24 seed skills, with the largest impact on Omni-MATH where static heuristics cannot cover Olympiad-level diversity. Removing the confidence gate has the smallest effect but pushes utilization to 91%, confirming its role as noise filter for borderline cases. As shown in Figure 3(b), both ARISE and GRPO start from the same baseline. Once Phase II activates, ARISE diverges as the library grows and useful skills are increasingly leveraged. The accuracy gap widens in tandem with library growth, providing direct evidence that policy improvement and library enrichment are mutually reinforcing. Notably, library size saturates in late Phase II while accuracy continues to improve, suggesting that later-stage gains come from the policy learning to select existing skills more effectively rather than from accumulating new ones.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: Figure 3: (a) Skill library statistics. (b) Policy library co-evolution. We presented ARISE, hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that unifies skill selection, generation, and policy optimization under single shared policy, enabling the skill library to co-evolve with the agent throughout training. Experiments on two base models across seven benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over GRPO-family baselines and memory-augmented methods. The ablation analysis reveals that the hierarchical reward is the primary driver of skill adoption, and that late-stage accuracy gains stem from improved selection over existing skills rather than continued library expansion, suggesting that library curation matters more than library size once sufficient skill repertoire is established. The current framework is evaluated exclusively on mathematical reasoning; extending ARISE to multi-tool agent tasks and code generation, where reusable skills take the form of executable procedures rather than reasoning heuristics, is promising direction for future work. 8 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: A Implementation Details
sections.content: A.1 Training Configuration and Hyperparameters All experiments are trained on the DeepScaleR dataset (Luo et al., 2025), comprising approximately 40K problem-answer pairs from AMC, AIME, MATH, and OlympiadBench, for 2,000 optimization steps. Training is conducted on A100-80GB GPUs using the GRPO objective (Eq. 6) with group size G=8. We set the per-device batch size to 4 with gradient accumulation over 2 steps, yielding an effective batch of 64 queries per policy update. The optimizer is AdamW with learning rate 1106, weight decay 0.01, and cosine learningrate schedule with 50 linear warm-up steps. The maximum generation length is 4,096 tokens for both solution rollouts O1, . . . , OG and the skill generation rollout OG+1. The clipping parameter ϵc in Eq. 6 is 0.2 for all methods. Phase spans the first Nw=500 steps with binary reward rtask {0, 1}. Phase II activates at step 501 with the hierarchical reward {0, 1, 2} and full skill selection. Following DAPO (Yu et al., 2025), we apply dynamic sampling to filter out zero-variance groups those where all trajectories receive the same rewardbefore advantage estimation, which stabilizes the gradient signal in both phases. Table 3 consolidates all ARISE-specific hyperparameters. Table 3: ARISE-specific hyperparameters. Parameters above the double rule are introduced by our method; those below are inherited from the GRPO training infrastructure. Hyperparameter Symbol Value Skill Selection (Manager) Softmax temperature ε-greedy exploration rate Confidence gate threshold Max skill tokens for scoring Skill Library Cache capacity Reservoir capacity Utility EMA coefficient Number of seed skills Skill Generation (OG+1) Generation temperature Top-p sampling Max new tokens Max skill document length Training Schedule Warm-up steps (Phase I) Skill utilization bonus Shared GRPO Infrastructure Group size Clipping parameter Learning rate Weight decay Max generation length σ ε δ Cc Cr β Mseed Nw rskill ϵc 1.0 0.1 0.35 128 10 100 0.9 5 0.7 0.95 192 220 chars 500 1.0 8 0.2 1106 0.01 4,096 tokens A.2 Complete Training Pipeline Each training step executes the following sequence, applicable to both phases with the differences noted inline: 11 Preprint. Under review. 1. Batch loading. Sample mini-batch of queries {q} D. 2. Skill selection (Phase II only). For each query, the manager scores all cache entries via Eq. 2, converts scores to selection distribution (Eq. 3), and samples skill zt. If maxk pk < δ, no skill is injected. 3. Prompt construction. Prepend the selected skill to the query following the injection format described in Appendix C.4. When no skill is selected, the query is presented without modification. 4. Solution rollouts. Generate trajectories τ1, . . . , τG from the current policy πθold . 5. Reward computation. Assign Ri = rtask per trajectory (see Appendix D.2). In + rskill Phase I, rskill = 0 for all i. 6. Dynamic sampling. Remove groups with zero reward variance before advantage estimation, following DAPO (Yu et al., 2025). 7. Advantage estimation and policy update. Compute group-relative advantages ˆAi via Eq. 5, compute log-probabilities under πθ and πθold , and update θ via LGRPO(θ) (Eq. 6). ˆAi > 0}. If 8. Skill generation (OG+1). Collect positive-advantage traces τ+ = {τi τ+ = , execute the summary rollout (Appendix D.1) to produce new skill mnew. 9. Library maintenance. Update the utility of the selected skill via EMA; insert mnew into the cache; execute EVICT/LOAD/DELETE operations as needed (Appendix C.2). Steps 23 are skipped in Phase I, and the reward in step 5 reduces to the binary signal rtask. Steps 89 execute in both phases, ensuring the library is populated before Phase II begins. A.3 Evaluation Protocol All results report average Pass@1 accuracy over 32 independent evaluation runs. In each run the model generates single solution per problem with greedy decoding (temperature 0, top-p = 1.0). At evaluation time the full ARISE pipeline is active: the manager selects skill from the cache via Eq. 23 with ε=0 (exploration disabled), and the worker generates the solution conditioned on the augmented prompt. If no skill exceeds the confidence threshold δ, the model solves the problem unaided. For in-distribution benchmarks (AMC 2023, AIME 2024&2025), answers are verified by exact numerical match. For Omni-MATH, we adopt the official evaluation protocol of Gao et al. (2024), which uses symbolic equivalence checking across its four domains. To ensure fair comparison, all baselines and ablation variants are evaluated with the same decoding configuration and the same 32-run averaging procedure. Formal Properties of the Evolving-Skill MDP This section provides additional formal details on the ES-MDP introduced in Section 3.1. B.1 State Space and Transition Structure The augmented state st = (qt, Mt) decomposes into an exogenous component qt (the query, sampled independently at each step) and an endogenous component Mt (the skill library, shaped by the agents preceding actions). This decomposition distinguishes the ES-MDP from standard MDP in two respects. First, the library transition is deterministic conditioned on the library management action : given the current library Mt and validated skill document mnew, the five management alib operations (Section 3.1) produce unique successor Mt+1. Stochasticity enters only through the policys generation of mnew via OG+1, not through the transition itself. Second, the library state Mt grows monotonically in information: new skills can be added, existing skills can be updated or relocated between tiers, and only reservoir entries meeting 12 Preprint. Under review. strict low-utility criteria are deleted. This monotonicity provides an implicit curriculum later training steps operate with richer library than earlier ones, enabling the policy to condition on increasingly specialized reasoning strategies. B.2 Factorization of the Hierarchical Policy The joint action probability in Eq. 1 factorizes into three sequential decisions. We expand each factor below. Manager: Skill Selection. The manager selects skill index zt from the cache Mc on query-skill relevance scores. The conditional distribution is: (cid:40)(1 ε) I[k = arg maxj pj] + ε I[k = ] if maxj pj δ otherwise (zt = qt, Mt) = πmgr θ Mc based (7) where pj = exp(sj/σ)/ exp(sj /σ) and zt = denotes the no-skill action. The confidence gate threshold δ ensures that the manager abstains when no candidate is sufficiently relevant, preventing the injection of misleading context. Worker: Solution Generation. Conditioned on the selected skill mzt (or an empty context when zt = ), the worker generates solution trace token by token: πwkr θ τt l=1 The skill document, when present, is prepended to the query within the prompt template, so that the worker attends to it through the standard causal attention mechanism without architectural modification. πθ(xt,l qt, mzt , xt,<l) (τt qt, mzt ) = (8) Manager: Skill Generation. After observing the rollout outcomes, the manager generates new skill document from positive-advantage traces: (9) When no positive-advantage trace exists (τ+ = ), the skill generation rollout is skipped and the library remains unchanged: Mt+1 = Mt. qt, Mt, τt) = πθ(mnew qt, τ+) I[τ+ = ] θ (alib πlib B.3 Advantage Estimation under the Hierarchical Reward The shift from binary reward rtask {0, 1} to the hierarchical reward {0, 1, 2} changes the structure of the group-relative advantage in precise way. Consider rollout group of size containing n2 trajectories with R=2, n1 with R=1, and n0 with R=0, where n0 + n1 + n2 = G. The group mean and standard deviation are: µG = n1 + 2n2 , (cid:115) σG = n1(1 µG)2 + n2(2 µG)2 + n0 µ2 The advantage for each reward level is then: ˆA(R=2) = ˆA(R=1) = ˆA(R=0) = 2 µG σG + ϵ 1 µG σG + ϵ µG σG + ϵ > 0 (sign depends on µG) < 0 (10) (11) (12) (13) Crucially, when µG > 1 (i.e., when the group contains more R=2 than R=0 trajectories), the advantage for R=1 becomes negative, meaning that correct-but-unaugmented solutions are actively down-weighted relative to skill-augmented ones. This mechanism provides an increasingly strong signal for skill utilization as training progresses and the proportion of successful skill-augmented trajectories grows. 13 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: C Skill Library Architecture
sections.content: C.1 Skill Document Schema and Seed Skills Every skill is stored as JSON document conforming to uniform five-field schema designed to ensure that log-probability scoring (Eq. 2) reflects semantic relevance rather than format variation. Table 4 specifies the constraints on each field. Table 4: Skill document JSON schema with field-level constraints. Field Max Length Description skill name problem type 40 chars key insight 160 chars method check 3 steps 100 chars Snake case identifier; must be non-empty One of: algebra, geometry, combinatorics, number theory, calculus, general Core mathematical idea explaining why the approach works 23 procedural steps (each 100 chars) Verification procedure; defaults to Substitute back to verify Total document Hard-capped at 220 characters Figure 4 shows representative generated skill. { "skill name": "exponential base matching", "problem type": "algebra", "key insight": "When both sides of an equation can be expressed as powers of the same base, set exponents equal", "method": [ "Rewrite each side with common base", "Set the exponents equal and solve", "Verify the solution satisfies the original" ], "check": "Substitute back into the original equation" } Figure 4: Example of generated skill document following the uniform schema. The cache is initialized with Mseed=5 seed skills encoding generic mathematical heuristics that are broadly applicable across problem types. Table 5 lists all seed skills and their key insights. Each seed follows the same JSON schema and serves as warm-start, ensuring that the skill selection mechanism has non-trivial candidates from the very first step of Phase II. C.2 Two-Tier Cache-Reservoir Management Mr The skill library Mt = Mc consists of cache Mc (capacity Cc=10) and reservoir Mr (capacity Cr=100). The cache is the active pool from which skills are scored and selected at each training step; the reservoir archives surplus skills for potential future promotion. Each library entry ek = (mk, uk) pairs skill document mk with scalar utility estimate uk R. Five management operations govern the library dynamics, executed in the order listed below at the end of each training step: UPDATE. The utility of the skill zt selected in the current step is updated via exponential moving average: uzt β uzt + (1 β) Rt, where β=0.9 and Rt is the hierarchical reward received by the trajectory. Skills not selected in this step retain their previous utility. 14 Preprint. Under review. Table 5: Seed skills used to initialize the cache at the beginning of training. Skill Name equation setup Algebra Problem Type Key Insight modular arithmetic check Number Theory case enumeration General symmetry exploitation General extremal principle General Translate word-problem quantities into variables and equations before solving Reduce expressions modulo small primes to constrain or verify integer solutions Systematically split into exhaustive cases and verify each independently Identify and leverage algebraic or geometric symmetry to simplify the problem Consider boundary or extremal configurations to establish bounds or find optima ADD. When the summary rollout OG+1 produces valid skill mnew (see Appendix D.1), it is inserted into the cache with initial utility u=0 and usage count 0. EVICT. If the cache size exceeds Cc after insertion, the entry with the lowest utility is moved to the reservoir, preserving its utility and usage metadata. LOAD. If any reservoir skill has utility estimate exceeding that of the lowest-utility cache entry, the two are swapped. This allows previously evicted skills to re-enter the active pool when they become relevant to the current training distribution. DELETE. Reservoir entries satisfying both conditionsutility below the 10th percentile of all reservoir utilities, and zero cumulative usageare permanently removed. The rationale behind the two-tier design is twofold. First, keeping the cache small (Cc=10) limits the computational cost of scoring all candidates via Eq. 2 at each step, while ensuring that only high-utility skills compete for selection. Second, the reservoir prevents premature discarding of skills that may regain relevance as the training distribution shifts. The coevolution evidence in Section 4.3 confirms that library size saturates in late Phase II while accuracy continues to improve, indicating that the LOAD/EVICT cycle refines the active pool rather than merely expanding it. C.3 Execution Order and Edge Cases The five operations always execute in the fixed order UPDATE ADD EVICT LOAD DELETE. This ordering ensures that (i) the utility of the selected skill reflects the most recent reward before any insertion or eviction occurs, (ii) newly added skill can immediately trigger an eviction if the cache is full, and (iii) the LOAD check runs after eviction so that just-evicted skill does not immediately re-enter. Several edge cases arise in practice: When OG+1 fails validation (see Appendix D.1), ADD is skipped and the remaining operations proceed on the unchanged cache. When the reservoir is empty, LOAD and DELETE are no-ops. When the confidence gate rejects all candidates (zt = ), UPDATE is skipped because no skill was selected, but ADD through DELETE still execute normally. When all trajectories receive the same reward (zero-variance group), dynamic sampling removes the group from advantage estimation, but the skill generation rollout OG+1 and library maintenance still proceed if τ+ = . C.4 Policy-Driven Skill Selection and Injection Skill selection in ARISE is performed by the policy πθ itself through conditional logprobability scoring, as described in Section 3.1. This appendix provides implementationlevel details. 15 Preprint. Under review. For each candidate mk Mc , the manager computes the score sk (Eq. 2) by feeding the concatenation of query qt and skill text mk through πθ and summing the per-token logprobabilities of mk. To limit computational cost, skill texts are truncated to 128 tokens during scoring; the full document is used for prompt injection. With cache of Cc=10 skills, this scoring step adds 10 128 = 1,280 extra tokens per query to the forward pass, modest overhead relative to the 4,096-token solution generation. The selected skill is injected by prepending it to the query with SKILL: prefix, formatted within the chat template as follows: <im start>user SKILL:{...skill JSON...} <math question> <im end> <im start>assistant The SKILL: prefix acts as structured delimiter that the model learns to attend to during Phase II. When the confidence gate rejects all candidates (maxk pk < δ), the query is presented without any prefix, and the trajectory is evaluated under the binary reward rtask {0, 1}. Scoring cost analysis. The log-probability scores are computed via single batched forward pass over Mc query-skill concatenations. Since skill documents are capped at 220 characters (approximately 6080 tokens after tokenization, truncated to 128 for scoring), and the query itself typically spans 50150 tokens, each scoring pass processes at most 10 278 = 2,780 tokens. On an A100-80GB GPU, this adds approximately 0.3 seconds per query, representing less than 8% of the total per-step wall time when including the G=8 solution rollouts.

sections.title: D Skill Generation and Reward
sections.content: D.1 Skill Distillation via Summary Rollout After the solution rollouts complete, the manager executes dedicated skill generation rollout OG+1, conditioned on the original query and the positive-advantage traces τ+ = ˆAi > 0}. This rollout distills the reasoning patterns embodied in τ+ into compact, {τi reusable skill document. Figure 5 shows the prompt template. The generated output undergoes four-stage validation pipeline before entering the library: 1. Extraction: isolate the JSON object from the raw generation, stripping any extraneous text or markdown fences; 2. Parsing: deserialize and verify that all five required fields exist and conform to their type constraints  (Table 4)  ; 3. Truncation: clip any field exceeding its character limit, and discard the document entirely if the total length exceeds 220 characters after clipping; 4. Insertion: add the validated skill to the cache via the ADD operation. If the generation fails to produce valid JSON (e.g., malformed brackets or missing fields), direct trace abstraction fallback constructs minimal skill from the first successful trace: {"skill name":"trace abstract", "key insight":"Solve by: {truncated response}"}. In practice, the primary summarization path succeeds in over 85% of attempts after the warm-up phase; the fallback rate drops further as the policy improves at structured generation during Phase II. ..., Design rationale. Two choices are worth highlighting. First, conditioning the summary on concrete successful traces τ+ turns skill generation from open-ended strategy induction into structured summarization, yielding more precise and transferable skills than unconditioned 16 Preprint. Under review. Summary Rollout Prompt Template You are skill distiller for math reasoning. Given one question and trajectories from the same rollout group, summarize ONE reusable skill. Question: {question} Group trajectories: [SUCCESS #1] {clipped response 1 (400 chars)} [SUCCESS #2] {clipped response 2 (400 chars)} Output MUST be valid JSON object and nothing else (no markdown/code fences). Schema: {"skill name", "problem type", "key insight", "method", "check"} Rules: - Be generic and transferable; do not copy specific numbers. - Keep the whole skill within 220 characters. - key insight is the most important field. - method must contain 2--3 concise steps. - Focus on improving correctness, not style. Figure 5: Prompt template used for the skill generation rollout OG+1. Up to two successful traces are included, each truncated to 400 characters. The generation uses temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95, and maximum of 192 new tokens. generation would. Second, the 220-character hard cap on document length ensures that every skill consumes comparable number of tokens, so that log-probability scores in Eq. 2 are dominated by semantic relevance rather than length artifacts. Without this cap, longer documents would accumulate lower (more negative) log-probability sums, biasing selection toward shorter, less informative skills regardless of their content. D.2 Hierarchical Reward Details The hierarchical reward = rtask + rskill {0, 1, 2} is assigned at the position of the last valid response token in each trajectory. Table 6 enumerates all four configurations. Table 6: Reward assignment by skill usage and task correctness. The bonus rskill is granted only when the agent both uses selected skill and solves the problem correctly, preventing the policy from being reinforced for skill usage that does not improve reasoning. Skill Used Task Correct rtask rskill No No Yes Yes No Yes No Yes 0 1 0 1 0 0 0 1 0 1 0 2 (incorrect) (correct, unaided) (incorrect despite skill) (correct with skill) The group-relative advantage (Eq. 5) computed over assigns trajectories with R=2 strictly higher advantage than those with R=1 whenever both outcomes coexist in the same group. Critically, incorrect trajectories receive R=0 regardless of skill usage, so the policy is never reinforced for applying irrelevant skills. The detailed advantage structure under different group compositions is analyzed in Appendix B.3. D.3 Information Gain Metric Beyond the scalar utility maintained per skill (updated via EMA as described in Appendix C.2), we track an information gain (IG) metric that measures how much skill 17 Preprint. Under review. text increases the policys confidence on known correct solutions: IG(mk) = τ+ (cid:34) log πθ (cid:0)τ+ mk, q, τ+ <t (cid:1) log πθ (cid:0)τ+ q, τ+ <t (cid:1) (cid:35) (14) Positive values indicate that conditioning on mk concentrates probability mass on correct reasoning steps. Computing Eq. 14 exactly requires two forward passes per (skill, trace) pair, which is prohibitively expensive at every step. We therefore maintain lightweight proxy updated online: (cid:99)IG(mk) = 1 2 (cid:16) rmk rglobal (cid:17) (cid:16) + 1 2 SRmk SRglobal (cid:17) (15) where rmk and SRmk denote the running average reward and success rate for trajectories using skill mk, and rglobal, SRglobal are the corresponding global statistics. The exact IG (Eq. 14) is computed every 100 steps for monitoring and stored alongside each skills metadata. The proxy IG serves primarily as diagnostic tool rather than training signal. It provides complementary perspective to the EMA utility: while utility tracks cumulative reward, IG isolates the causal contribution of the skill by comparing performance with and without conditioning. Skills with high utility but low or negative IG are likely correlated with easy problemsthey are selected when the query happens to be straightforwardrather than causally contributing to the solution. Identifying such skills helps interpret the librarys evolution and may inform future work on more selective library management strategies.

sections.title: E Extended Analysis
sections.content: E.1 Hyperparameter Sensitivity We study the sensitivity of ARISE to four key hyperparameters on Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, evaluating Pass@1 accuracy on AIME 2024 with all other settings fixed at their defaults. Table 7 reports the results. Table 7: Hyperparameter sensitivity on AIME 2024 (Qwen3-4B, Pass@1 %). Default values are underlined. Each row varies single parameter while holding the rest at default. Confidence gate δ Accuracy Skill Use (%) Exploration ε Accuracy Cache size Cc Accuracy Warm-up Nw Accuracy 0.15 55.2 89.3 0.0 55.7 5 55.5 200 54.8 0.25 55.9 81. 0.05 56.0 10 56.4 350 55.6 0.35 56.4 73.0 0.10 56.4 20 56. 500 56.4 0.45 56.1 58.2 0.20 55.8 0.55 55.0 41.7 0.30 54.9 50 55.3 750 56.2 1000 55.5 Confidence gate δ. This threshold governs the trade-off between skill utilization and selection precision. At δ=0.15, nearly 90% of rollouts receive skill injection, but the liberal threshold admits marginally relevant skills that can introduce misleading context, reducing accuracy by 1.2 points. Conversely, δ=0.55 suppresses skill usage to 42%, forfeiting the benefit of the hierarchical reward and underperforming the full model by 1.4 points. The default at δ=0.35 injects skills in 73% of rollouts while maintaining high selection precision. Exploration rate ε. Performance is robust across ε [0.05, 0.15]. Pure exploitation (ε=0) slightly underperforms the default because the manager never evaluates under-explored skills, potentially missing useful entries that recently entered the cache. Beyond ε=0.2, random injection increasingly dominates, degrading accuracy as irrelevant skills corrupt the reasoning context. 18 Preprint. Under review. Cache size Cc. The default of 10 balances diversity against scoring cost. Halving it to 5 limits the available skill repertoire, while expanding to 50 dilutes the active pool with low-utility entries and increases the per-step scoring overhead by 5 without commensurate accuracy gains. Warm-up length Nw. Shorter warm-ups (Nw=200) yield the largest accuracy deficit (1.6 points), because Phase II begins with both weak base policy and an underpopulated library. Excessively long warm-ups (Nw=1000) delay the onset of co-evolution, consuming half the training budget before skill-augmented learning activates. The default of 500 steps provides sufficient base policy and library of approximately 5080 skills before Phase II begins. E.2 Skill Library Evolution Table 8 illustrates how the skill library evolves qualitatively during training by presenting representative skills at three stages: initialization (seed skills), mid-training (step 1,000), and late training (step 2,000). Table 8: Representative skill documents at different training stages, illustrating the progression from generic heuristics to domain-specific techniques. Stage Skill Name equation setup Type Algebra case enumeration General Seed Seed Step 1k modular residue analysisNum. Theory Step 1k generating function setupCombin. Step 2k vieta root reconstructionAlgebra Step 2k angle chasing with inscribed Geometry Key Insight Translate word-problem quantities into variables and equations before solving into exhaustive Systematically split cases and verify each independently Reduce expressions modulo to constrain integer solutions before full computation Encode counting sequence as polynomial coefficients and extract the target term Use Vietas formulas to express symmetric functions of roots without solving the polynomial In cyclic quadrilaterals, the inscribed angle theorem converts arc relations to angle equations Several trends emerge. First, early generated skills (steps 500800) tend to be broad heuristics resembling the seedsstrategies for setting up equations or checking parity. As the policy encounters harder problems and the hierarchical reward reinforces successful skill usage, mid-training skills become increasingly specialized to specific domains (Number Theory, Combinatorics). By late training, the library contains highly targeted techniques (Vietas formulas, inscribed angle chasing) that transfer effectively to Olympiad-level problems in Omni-MATH. This specialization trajectory provides qualitative explanation for the disproportionately large out-of-distribution gains reported in Table 1: the evolving library accumulates domain-specific reasoning patterns that the base policy alone does not develop through standard GRPO training. Second, the co-evolution evidence from Section 4.3 shows that library size plateaus around step 1,500 while accuracy continues to rise, confirming that late-stage improvements stem from better selection over existing skills rather than from accumulating new ones. The LOAD/EVICT cycle effectively curates the active cache, promoting specialized skills and retiring those that have become redundant as the policy internalizes their strategies. 19 Preprint. Under review. E.3 Phase Transition Dynamics The transition from Phase to Phase II at step Nw+1 introduces two simultaneous changes: the activation of skill selection and the switch from binary to hierarchical reward. To understand their relative contributions, we examine training dynamics around this boundary. During the first 1020 steps of Phase II, skill utilization rises rapidly from 0% to approximately 4050% as the manager begins injecting seed skills and early generated skills. This initial surge is driven primarily by the ε-greedy exploration term rather than confident selection, as the policy has not yet learned to discriminate among candidates. Over the subsequent 100200 steps, the confidence gate increasingly filters out low-relevance injections and the utilization rate stabilizes around 6575%, reflecting genuine query-skill matching. The reward distribution also shifts notably. In Phase I, the advantage landscape is binary: trajectories are either correct or incorrect. In Phase II, the introduction of R=2 creates three-level hierarchy that produces more informative gradient signals. Groups containing mix of R=0, R=1, and R=2 trajectories have higher reward variance, which amplifies the advantage magnitude and provides stronger learning signal per gradient step compared to the binary case. This partly explains the accelerated accuracy improvement observed in the first 200 steps of Phase II (Figure 1, middle panel). E.4 Token Overhead Analysis Prepending skill documents to the prompt incurs additional tokens. Table 9 compares average per-problem token counts on AIME 2024 across methods. Table 9: Token overhead comparison on AIME 2024 (Qwen3-4B). Overhead is computed as (total tokensmethod total tokensGRPO) / total tokensGRPO 100%, where total tokens = prompt + response. Method Prompt Response Total Overhead GRPO DAPO GSPO EvolveR + GRPO ARISE + GRPO 312 312 312 489 378 2,847 2,903 2,871 2,921 2, 3,159 3,215 3,183 3,410 3,254 +1.8% +0.8% +7.9% +3.0% ARISE adds an average of 66 prompt tokens per problem (the injected skill document), direct consequence of the 220-character hard cap on skill length. Despite this modest overhead, ARISE yields 2.9-point average accuracy gain on Omni-MATH over GRPO  (Table 1)  , achieving the best accuracy-to-overhead trade-off among all methods. By comparison, EvolveR injects longer experience narratives, incurring 7.9% overhead for only 0.7-point Omni-MATH gain. This efficiency advantage is visualized in Figure 1 (bottom-right), where ARISE occupies the Pareto-optimal position in the accuracy-gain-versus-overhead space. DAPO and GSPO have zero prompt overhead (no skill injection) but still show non-zero total overhead due to slightly longer response lengths caused by their modified sampling strategies. The response length of ARISE (2,876 tokens) is comparable to vanilla GRPO (2,847 tokens), confirming that skill injection does not inflate the solution lengththe injected skill guides the reasoning strategy without encouraging more verbose output. E.5 Computational Cost Breakdown Table 10 reports the wall-clock time per training step on 8A100-80GB GPUs, broken down by component. The dominant overhead comes from the skill generation rollout OG+1 (4.8s, or 9.4% of total time), which generates single sequence of up to 192 tokens. Skill scoring adds 2.4s (4.7%) due to the Mc =10 forward passes for log-probability computation. Library maintenance is negligible (<0.2%). The total per-step overhead of 16.6% is modest given the consistent 20 Preprint. Under review. Table 10: Per-step wall-clock time breakdown on 8A100-80GB (Qwen3-4B, G=8). Percentages are relative to GRPO baseline. Component GRPO ARISE Solution rollouts (O1, . . . , OG) Reward computation Advantage estimation & gradient update Skill scoring & selection (Phase II) Skill generation (OG+1) Library maintenance Total per step Overhead vs. GRPO 38.2s 0.4s 12.6s 51.2s 39.1s 0.5s 12.8s 2.4s 4.8s 0.1s 59.7s +16.6% accuracy gains observed across all benchmarks, and could be further reduced by batching the scoring passes or caching log-probabilities for skills that remain unchanged between consecutive steps.
